1: Psalm 17 is a royal prayer — the king petitions for divine vindication against unjust enemies, and the language is consistently forensic throughout. "Hear a just cause (ṣedeq), O LORD; listen to my cry; give ear to my prayer from lips without deceit (mirmāh)" — the king approaches God as a plaintiff before the divine court, claiming the justice of his cause and the sincerity of his petition. "Lips without deceit" is the claim of complete rhetorical integrity — no manipulation, no flattery, no hidden agenda (contrasting with Ps 12:2-4's corrupt speech). The divine court is the final court of appeal when human courts fail.
2: "Let my vindication (mišpāṭî) come from you; let your eyes behold what is just (mêšārîm)" — mišpāṭ is both legal process and verdict: the psalmist requests that the divine judge render a fair decision. "Behold what is just" — the impartial divine gaze (cf. Ps 9:8; 11:4) is exactly what the psalmist invites. The petition is not for special favor but for honest judgment, confident that honest judgment will vindicate. This is the radical confidence of Ps 1's theology: the righteous need only ask for divine scrutiny, not divine partiality.
3: "You have tested my heart; you have examined me through the night; you have tried me (ṣĕraftanî) and found nothing" — the refining metaphor again (cf. Ps 12:6). Ṣāraf is the metallurgist's verb: to smelt, assay, refine metal in fire. God has applied the refiner's examination and found no impurity. "Through the night" — either the night of a temple vigil during which this prayer was offered (a common practice; cf. Ps 16:7 and 17:15), or the ongoing night of trial and suffering through which God has been silently watching and testing the psalmist's integrity.
4: "By the word of your lips I have avoided the ways of the violent (pārîṣ)" — the claim of Torah-shaped formation. "By the word of your lips (biḏbar śĕpātêkā)" — the psalmist has shaped his conduct by divine speech, not by cultural convention. Pārîṣ (violent, destructive) describes the social predator of Pss 10, 12, 14. The word of God functions as counter-formation, creating a person whose character resists the ambient culture of violence — an ethic shaped from above, not from below.
5: "My steps have kept to your paths (ʾăšûrōtay); my feet have not stumbled (lōʾ-māṭû)" — ʾāšar (to step, go straight) and nāṭāh (to turn aside, deviate) form the contrast. The paths of God are the covenant way of Torah; stumbling is the standard metaphor for moral failure in wisdom literature (Prov 3:23; 4:12; Isa 59:10). The path metaphor in the Psalms (Pss 1, 23, 25, 27) reflects the ancient understanding of covenant life as a journey on a defined road — not a series of isolated ethical decisions but a sustained directional movement.
6: "I call on you, for you will answer me, O God; turn your ear (haṭṭēh-ʾoznĕkā) to me; hear my words" — the confidence of petition grounded in covenant relationship. "Turn your ear" is a physical idiom: the judge or patron physically turning to give ear signals willingness to receive a petition. In ancient Mediterranean patronage, the ability to get access to a powerful person's ear was the key social currency. The psalmist claims access to the greatest patron — not by social standing but by covenant — with the confidence that YHWH will incline toward those who call on him.
7: "Display your wonderful steadfast love (hapleʾ ḥăsādêkā), O Savior (môšîaʿ) of those who seek refuge from their enemies by your right hand" — Hapleʾ (make wonderful, distinguish, make visible) — make the ḥeseḏ conspicuously evident. Môšîaʿ is the Hiphil participle of yāšaʿ (save): the one who saves, the deliverer. This is the root of yēšûaʿ (Joshua/Jesus) — "the LORD saves." The petition invokes God's covenant name and saving character simultaneously, asking for a visible demonstration of ḥeseḏ against the background of military threat.
8: "Guard me as the apple of your eye (ʾîšôn bat-ʿāyin); hide me in the shadow of your wings" — two protection images. (1) ʾIšôn (apple of the eye) literally means "little man" — the tiny image one sees reflected in another's pupil; by extension, what is most precious and most carefully guarded. Cf. Deut 32:10 — God keeps Israel "as the apple of his eye." (2) Shadow of wings (bĕṣēl kĕnāpêkā) — God protecting as a bird covers its young (Deut 32:11; Ruth 2:12; Pss 36:7; 57:1; 61:4; 91:4). Both images express the intimate, attentive protection of what is most valued.
9: "From the wicked who assail me, from my deadly enemies (ʾōyĕbay bĕnepeš — enemies against the soul/life) who encircle me" — the military encirclement image. In ancient siege warfare, to encircle a city cut off escape and supply — the total vulnerability of the besieged. The phrase "enemies against the soul/life" specifies those who seek not mere defeat but complete annihilation — nepeš (life, soul, the person) itself is what they target. The psalmist is not dealing with political opposition but with enemies who want him utterly destroyed.
10: "Their hearts are closed to compassion (ḥelbāmô sāgerû — their fat encloses them); their mouths speak with arrogance" — "their fat" (ḥēleb) refers to the rich fat around vital organs. To be enclosed in fat is to be insulated against any perception of others' suffering — self-satisfied, impervious. This physiological metaphor for moral imperviousness recurs in the Prophets: Isaiah 6:10 (heart fat, ears heavy, eyes closed); Jeremiah 5:28 (the wicked grow fat and sleek). The sequence is anatomically precise: a fat heart produces an arrogant mouth.
11: "Now they surround our every step; they watch for a chance to bring us to the ground (lintôt bāʾāreṣ — to stretch us out on the earth)" — the shift from "me" (my steps, my feet — vv. 5, 11) to "us" (bring us to the ground) suggests the prayer has a communal dimension: the king petitions for himself and his community together. The watching for a moment of vulnerability (ʿênêhem yāšîtû — their eyes they set) is the predatory gaze scanning for the opportune moment, the same gaze as the lion crouching in Ps 10:9-10.
12: "He is like a lion (ʾaryēh) eager for prey, like a young lion (kĕpîr) crouching in ambush" — the dual lion image, recalling Ps 10:9. ʾAryēh (mature lion) and kĕpîr (young male lion in his prime — the most dangerous) emphasize both power and cunning at their peak. ANE royal iconography regularly depicted enemies as lions to be defeated — the king's prowess was demonstrated by subduing both wild animals and foreign enemies. David's lion-killing (1 Sam 17:34-37) established his credentials as the one capable of overcoming such threats; here the enemy takes the lion's role against the psalmist.
13: "Arise, LORD! Confront him! Bring him down! Rescue me from the wicked by your sword" — five imperatives in rapid succession. The battle-cry qûmāh yhwh (Arise, LORD) echoes Num 10:35 (the ark's departure formula), Pss 3:7; 7:6; 9:19; 10:12. The urgency and compression of five consecutive imperatives mirrors the panic of immediate threat. "Your sword (ḥarbĕkā)" — God as divine warrior brandishing his weapon is widespread in ANE iconography and biblical tradition: Deut 32:41-43 ("I will sharpen my flashing sword"); Isa 66:16; Ezek 21.
14: "By your hand, LORD, from those whose portion (ḥeleq) is in this present life" — the enemies are "men whose portion is in this life" — contrasting sharply with Ps 16:5 (YHWH as the psalmist's portion). The wicked have no heavenly inheritance; this world's goods are their entire allotment. "Their belly is filled with what you store up" — God is giving them temporal prosperity (their "portion"), yet this is precisely what makes them dangerous. Their children will inherit what remains, but the inheritance is finite. The contrast with the psalmist's eternal inheritance (Ps 16:11) frames the psalm's two competing life-orientations.
15: "As for me, I will behold your face in righteousness (ṣedeq); when I awake I will be satisfied with your likeness (tĕmûnātĕkā)" — the closing verse sets the ultimate contrast with v. 14. The wicked have their entire portion in this life; the psalmist's portion is the vision of God. "Beholding the face" is the language of temple access — to see God's face in the sanctuary (Pss 11:7; 42:2; 63:2). Tĕmûnāh (likeness, form) suggests a vision of divine glory — the manifestation of God's presence, not direct access to the divine essence (cf. Num 12:8, where Moses sees the tĕmûnāh of YHWH). "When I awake" carries both the immediate sense of dawn after a night vigil and an eschatological overtone resonating with Ps 16:10's resurrection hope.
